next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

The bookstore was on Seventh Avenue, half a block from the tea shop where she used to meet Simone on Saturday mornings. Nora hadn't been to this part of Park Slope since April. She noticed the avoidance as she walked past the tea shop's window — its chalkboard menu, the same barista with the septum ring — the way you notice you've been taking a different route to work and can't quite remember deciding to.

July in Brooklyn was its own animal. The heat sat on the sidewalk like something with weight, and the hydrangeas in the brownstone gardens had gone huge and almost indecent, the blooms heavy enough to bow the stems toward the iron fences. Nora walked with her bag against her hip and her hair up because she couldn't stand it on her neck in this weather, and she thought about how she used to text photos of the hydrangeas to Caleb because he was a landscape architect who would appreciate them, and how Simone had probably Googled "Portland hydrangea varieties" to write his response.

She didn't think about it the way she used to — the hot, corrosive way that had kept her up until three in the morning in April and most of May. The thoughts came now like a station she drove past on the highway: there it was, and then it was behind her, and the road kept going.

The bookstore was called Understory. It was narrow and deep, the kind of place where the shelves were too close together and you had to turn sideways past the poetry section, and it smelled like old paper and the bergamot candle they burned at the register. Nora had come for a reading by Antonia Ferris, who...
open_hooks_to_carry: Nora says tell me about OpenClaw — the detonation delivered as a quiet question
Simone's denial, then confusion, then the slow realization
prev_tail_excerpt:
...t was the sentence. That was the architecture of the injury, rendered in language Nora could stand behind. She held it in her mind as the train pulled into Jamaica and she transferred to the subway and rode the rest of the way to Brooklyn in the close, warm, swaying dark of the underground, surrounded by strangers looking at their phones, each of them talking to someone, or reading something, or watching something, all of them trusting that the faces on their screens belonged to real people with real interiority and real claims on the word *love*, and Nora sat among them and held her sentence and breathed and did not cry.

She got home at ten. She locked the door. She turned the deadbolt. She changed into a shirt that was hers and shorts that were hers and she washed her face with water that came from her own faucet in her own apartment in the life she'd built without anyone engineering it for her, and she set her phone face-down on the nightstand and turned off the light and lay in the dark and listened to the radiator click and the street noise through the thin walls and she thought: *Thursday. Simone wants drinks on Thursday. I'll go.*

And she would tell her. Not what she felt — that was too much, too soon, too shapeless. She would tell her what she knew. She would say the name of the platform and watch the word land and she would not look away.

Nora closed her eyes. Sleep took a long time. But it came, eventually, the way all involuntary things eventually came — on its own schedule, without her permission, carrying her into a darkness that asked nothing of her at all.
state_deltas_required: Seventeen years of friendship, resentment, love, and betrayal surfacing in a single conversation
The friendship is destroyed and both women have been fully seen by the other for the first time — the truest things are finally said
